agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"CEO described a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The description is high-level and intentionally vague — typical of an all-hands vision pitch rather than a product spec. Key goals: competitiveness, profitability, automation, and safety guardrails.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"A measure of current delivery request volume relative to capacity (definition not specified — this is a gap)"},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing"},{"term":"Real time","definition":"Price adjustments occur dynamically as demand changes, not on a batch/scheduled basis"},{"term":"Fully automated","definition":"No manual human intervention required for price adjustments during normal operation"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"When demand increases, delivery prices must increase","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy encoding the core pricing strategy. A VP could decide to hold prices flat during demand spikes for promotional reasons.","testable_assertion":"Given demand metric rises above baseline, when a new delivery price is calculated, then the price is higher than the price at baseline demand"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"When demand decreases, delivery prices must decrease","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Business policy — the inverse pricing relationship. Could be overridden by a minimum profitability policy.","testable_assertion":"Given demand metric drops below baseline, when a new delivery price is calculated, then the price is lower than the price at baseline demand"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Price adjustments must occur in real time (not batch or delayed)","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"An operational requirement, not a data integrity constraint. The business could decide to batch updates every 5 minutes and still consider it acceptable.","testable_assertion":"Given demand conditions change, when a price recalculation is triggered, then the new price is available to customers within the system's defined real-time threshold"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The pricing system must be fully automated with no manual intervention required for standard price adjustments","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Operational policy. The business could add manual overrides or approval steps without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given demand changes occur, when prices are recalculated, then no human approval step is required before the new price takes effect"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Guardrails must exist to prevent prices from reaching extreme values (upper and/or lower bounds)","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without guardrails, the system could produce prices that are negative, zero, or astronomically high — violating basic pricing integrity. A price of $0 or -$5 for delivery is a corrupt state. A price of $50,000 for a local delivery is a corrupt state. The existence of bounds is a system integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given any demand level (including extreme spikes or crashes), when a price is calculated, then the resulting price falls within the configured minimum and maximum bounds"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Prices must maintain competitiveness — the system should not systematically price above market rates","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Business strategy goal, not a data integrity constraint. What counts as 'competitive' is a policy judgment that changes with market conditions.","testable_assertion":"Given stable market conditions, when prices are calculated, then prices remain within a competitive range relative to market benchmarks"},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"Prices must maintain profitability — the system should not price below cost","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Pricing below cost on every delivery is not a sustainable state. While occasional below-cost pricing can be strategic, the system must have a cost floor to prevent systematic loss-making. A price that doesn't cover variable cost is a corrupt pricing state.","testable_assertion":"Given any demand level including extreme lows, when a price is calculated, then the price is at or above the configured minimum cost floor"},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"The relationship between demand and price must be monotonic — higher demand must never produce a lower price than lower demand, all else being equal","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If higher demand produces lower prices, the pricing function is mathematically corrupt and violates the fundamental business intent. This is a logical integrity constraint on the pricing algorithm itself.","testable_assertion":"Given two demand levels D1 > D2 with all other inputs equal, when prices P1 and P2 are calculated, then P1 >= P2"}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"Demand is measured above the normal baseline threshold","when":"The system calculates a new delivery price","then":"The price is higher than the baseline price by an amount proportional to demand increase"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"Demand is measured below the normal baseline threshold","when":"The system calculates a new delivery price","then":"The price is lower than the baseline price by an amount proportional to demand decrease"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Demand spikes to an extreme level","when":"The system calculates a new delivery price","then":"The price does not exceed the configured maximum price guardrail"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Demand drops to near-zero","when":"The system calculates a new delivery price","then":"The price does not fall below the configured minimum price guardrail (cost floor)"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"Demand conditions change","when":"A price recalculation is triggered","then":"The new price is applied without any manual human approval step"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"Demand conditions change","when":"A price recalculation is triggered","then":"The updated price is available to customers within the defined real-time latency threshold"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand drops to zero (no orders at all)","expected_behavior":"Price should hit the configured floor/minimum, not drop to zero or negative"},{"scenario":"Demand spikes beyond any historically observed level","expected_behavior":"Price should hit the configured ceiling/maximum, not grow unbounded"},{"scenario":"Demand oscillates rapidly (e.g., flash spike then immediate drop)","expected_behavior":"System should avoid price whiplash — unclear if dampening/smoothing is required (ambiguity)"},{"scenario":"Two simultaneous orders see different prices due to real-time recalculation mid-request","expected_behavior":"Unclear — should a quoted price be locked for a window, or can it change between quote and confirmation?"},{"scenario":"Cost of delivery changes (fuel prices, driver shortage) while demand is low","expected_behavior":"Unclear — does the system only respond to demand, or also to supply-side cost changes?"},{"scenario":"Competitor drops prices dramatically","expected_behavior":"Unclear — 'competitive' was mentioned but no mechanism for market-aware pricing was specified"},{"scenario":"Guardrail bounds are misconfigured (min > max)","expected_behavior":"System must reject invalid guardrail configuration rather than produce undefined behavior"},{"scenario":"Regulatory price gouging laws apply during emergencies","expected_behavior":"Unaddressed — dynamic pricing during emergencies may violate price gouging regulations in some jurisdictions"}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing can significantly increase revenue during peak demand (surge pricing proven in ride-sharing to boost revenue 20-40%). However, miscalibrated guardrails risk either leaving money on the table (ceiling too low) or driving customers to competitors (ceiling too high). Below-cost pricing during low demand periods is a direct margin risk.","operational":"Fully automated system reduces pricing team overhead but introduces operational complexity: monitoring, guardrail configuration, demand signal reliability, and incident response for pricing anomalies. Real-time requirement implies infrastructure investment in low-latency systems.","user":"Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Surge pricing is well-understood in ride-sharing but may be novel in delivery — customer education needed. Price fairness perception is a significant UX concern.","risk":"High risk from regulatory exposure (price gouging laws), reputational damage (perceived unfairness), and algorithmic errors (pricing bugs could be very costly at scale in real time). Guardrail misconfiguration is a critical operational risk.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Will see variable delivery prices — may perceive unfairness during high-demand periods. Need transparency on why prices change."},{"group":"Delivery drivers/couriers","impact":"Higher demand pricing may affect their compensation model or dispatch priority — unclear if driver pay is linked to dynamic price."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue team","impact":"New revenue optimization lever but also new complexity in forecasting and margin analysis."},{"group":"Operations/Pricing team","impact":"Must configure and monitor guardrails, demand signals, and pricing algorithm performance."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Must evaluate price gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and disclosure requirements per jurisdiction."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will receive complaints about price variability — need training and escalation paths for pricing disputes."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement with almost no specificity. Critical parameters are undefined: what constitutes 'demand,' what the guardrail values are, how 'real time' is defined, what 'competitive' and 'profitable' mean quantitatively, whether supply-side factors matter, and whether regulatory constraints apply. The word 'probably' before guardrails further weakens confidence. This is a pitch, not a spec.","high_confidence_areas":["Core concept: prices go up with demand, down with low demand","System must be automated","Some form of guardrails/bounds will exist"],"low_confidence_areas":["Definition and measurement of 'demand' — is it order volume, order volume vs capacity, geographic density, time-based?","Guardrail values and whether they are per-region, per-service-type, or global","Whether 'real time' means sub-second, seconds, or minutes","How competitiveness is measured or enforced","Whether supply-side costs (fuel, driver availability) factor into pricing","Price locking semantics — is a quoted price guaranteed for a window?","Dampening/smoothing behavior for rapid demand fluctuations","Regulatory compliance requirements across jurisdictions","Whether driver compensation is affected by dynamic pricing","Manual override capability despite 'fully automated' description"],"assumptions":["'Demand' refers to delivery request volume relative to some baseline or capacity measure","Guardrails include both an upper and lower bound on price","The system prices individual deliveries, not bulk/contract pricing","There is a base/reference price that the system adjusts up or down","The CEO's 'probably' on guardrails will resolve to 'definitely' — no sane system ships without them"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Demand' is never defined — it could mean raw order volume, volume relative to courier capacity, geographic concentration, time-window density, or a composite metric. The entire pricing algorithm depends on this definition.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Product","Operations","Finance"],"options":["Define demand as raw order volume per time window","Define demand as order volume relative to available courier capacity","Define demand as a composite signal including geography, time, and capacity"],"recommendation":"Demand should be defined as order volume relative to available courier capacity in a geographic zone and time window — this captures both the demand and supply side, which is what actually drives pricing pressure."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Guardrails' are mentioned with 'probably' qualifier and no specifics — unclear what bounds exist, whether they are absolute price limits, percentage caps on adjustment, per-region, per-service-type, or global.","affected_stakeholders":["Product","Legal","Finance","Customers"],"options":["Absolute min/max price per delivery","Percentage cap on adjustment from base price (e.g., max 3x base)","Per-region configurable bounds","Combination of absolute and percentage limits"],"recommendation":"Implement both absolute bounds (no delivery below $X, none above $Y) AND percentage caps on adjustment from base price, configurable per region — this provides defense in depth."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Real time' is undefined — could mean sub-second, seconds, minutes, or per-order recalculation. This has major architectural and infrastructure implications.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Product","Operations"],"options":["Sub-second recalculation on every order","Recalculation every N seconds/minutes","Event-driven recalculation when demand crosses thresholds"],"recommendation":"Define a target latency SLA (e.g., price reflects demand conditions no older than 60 seconds) rather than a vague 'real time' requirement."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Competitive but also profitable' are potentially conflicting goals with no priority or quantitative definition. At what point does profitability override competitiveness or vice versa?","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Product","Executive team"],"options":["Profitability always wins — never price below cost floor regardless of competitors","Competitiveness wins within a margin band — match competitors down to a minimum margin","Configurable priority that varies by market maturity"],"recommendation":"Establish a hard cost floor (profitability invariant) and a soft competitiveness target — profitability is the constraint, competitiveness is the optimization objective within that constraint."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"CEO said 'probably' regarding guardrails — this is not a firm commitment. If guardrails are optional, the system could produce extreme prices.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Legal","Executive team"],"options":["Guardrails are mandatory and always active","Guardrails are optional and configurable","Guardrails are mandatory but values are configurable"],"recommendation":"Guardrails must be mandatory and always active — no responsible pricing system ships without them. Values should be configurable but existence is non-negotiable."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
